preFix.title: Installing a home EV charger
preFix.subtitle: What to consider before commissioning a home charge point
preFix.excerpt: A dedicated home charge point provides faster, safer, and smarter charging than a three-pin plug. The decisions involve charger type, smart functionality, solar integration, and electrical supply capacity. This is the decision guide.
preFix.sourceNotes: OZEV (Office for Zero Emission Vehicles) EV chargepoint grant eligibility guidance (2024). BS 7671 IET Wiring Regulations for EV charge point installation. Energy Networks Association guidance on single-phase vs three-phase supply for EV charging.

content.text: A standard three-pin plug delivers 2.3 kW to a vehicle. A dedicated 7.4 kW home charge point charges three times faster and is built to handle continuous overnight load cycles safely. For most domestic use, a 7.4 kW single-phase unit is enough. Three-phase 22 kW units are available but most home electrical supplies are single-phase. And most EVs charge at a maximum of 7.4 kW AC regardless.

Tethered units have a fixed cable permanently attached. Convenient if one vehicle uses the charger. Untethered units have a socket; the driver connects their own cable. Better if multiple vehicles with different cable types will use the charger. Both types deliver the same kWh rate.

Smart chargers connect to the internet. You can set them to charge during off-peak periods such as Octopus Go overnight rates. They can pause during peak grid demand or boost when your solar PV makes spare power. Since 2022. All new home charge points sold in Great Britain must have smart features under the EV Smart Charging rules.

A solar-integrated smart charger measures surplus generation via a CT clamp and dynamically adjusts the charge rate to consume exactly the surplus rather than importing from the grid. This requires a minimum surplus of around 1.4 kW to activate the boost mode. Below that threshold, the car charges at the minimum rate or waits. In practice, solar-boosted EV charging contributes most in summer afternoons when generation exceeds household demand.

A 7.4 kW charge point draws 32 amps on a single-phase supply. Most UK homes have a 60 to 100 amp main fuse. Adding 32 amps continuous load to an otherwise fully loaded supply can trip the main fuse. An OZEV-approved installer will assess the existing supply capacity as part of the survey. If the supply is marginal. Load management (limiting the charge rate to keep total draw within the fuse rating) is included in most modern smart charger firmware.

An OZEV-approved installer must fit the unit if you want the OZEV grant. As of 2024 the grant is open to renters and flat owners. Homeowners with solar PV may also qualify. The installer registers the work and issues an OZEV certificate.

attrs.body: The financial saving from a home charge point depends primarily on the electricity tariff, not the hardware. Overnight cheap-rate tariffs charge around 7 to 9p per kWh versus the standard 24p rate. For a driver covering 10,000 miles per year in a car consuming 4 miles per kWh. The difference between overnight cheap-rate and standard-rate charging is about 400 kWh per year. A smart charger that automatically uses cheap-rate periods pays back faster than the hardware cost alone suggests.
attrs.title: Overnight tariffs and the financial case

initialFindings.message: tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
